coredata.dc:title: Reusable red emission carbon dots based smartphone sensing platform for three-mode on-site real-time detection of alcohol content
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chenzhao
Li, Junxian
Wang, Xiujuan
Zhao, Zhenjie
Yao, Runxiang
Jiang, Yuhao
Wei, Shanshan
Wang, Zhibing
Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: A three-mode fluorescence sensing platform using reusable red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) as a fluorescent sensor was developed for rapid and highly sensitive detection of alcohol content in alcoholic beverages. The R-CDs prepared using ionic liquids as precursors exhibited excellent dispersion and red fluorescence characteristics in ethanol. Moreover, the fluorescence could be quenched due to their aggregation in water. Based on the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) mechanism, a new fluorescence assay for the detection of alcohol content was established with a limit of detection as low as 0.12 %. This sensing platform possessed the advantages of low cost, portability, and simplicity of operation, and laid a further foundation for the on-site detection application of R-CDs in the field of sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission carbon dots
Fluorescence sensing platform
Three-mode
Ionic liquid
Alcohol content

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: R-CDs- Smartphone detection platform for alcohol content
Determination of alcohol content by R-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)TEM and HR-TEM image, (B) particle size distribution, (C) XRD pattern, and (D) FT-IR spectrum of the R-CDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of R-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1 s, (C) N 1 s, (D) O 1 s, (E) F 1 s, and (F) B 1 s.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the R-CDs. Inset: pictures under white light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (B) Fluorescence spectra with the excitation wavelength increasing from 530 to 590 nm.

(A) Status of R-CDs in ethanol (left) and water (right). (B) UV-Vis absorption spectra of R-CDs before and after the addition of water. (C) Zeta potential of R-CDs in ethanol and water. (D) TEM images of the R-CDs in ethanol and water.
(A) Effect of different interfering substances. (B) Effects of alcohol content (0–100 %) on the fluorescence intensity. Inset is the relationship between fluorescence intensity and alcohol content.
Photographs of R-CD solutions with different alcohol contents under light (A) and a 365 nm UV lamp (B). Linearity of R/B with alcohol content under light (C) and under a 365 nm UV lamp (D).
Synthesis and application of the R-CDs.

head.ce:title: Reusable red emission carbon dots based smartphone sensing platform for three-mode on-site real-time detection of alcohol content

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are actively engaged in research and are committed to advancing knowledge in the field of analytical chemistry, particularly in the development and application of optical sensors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A three-mode fluorescence sensing platform using reusable red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) as a fluorescent sensor was developed for rapid and highly sensitive detection of alcohol content in alcoholic beverages. The R-CDs prepared using ionic liquids as precursors exhibited excellent dispersion and red fluorescence characteristics in ethanol. Moreover, the fluorescence could be quenched due to their aggregation in water. Based on the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) mechanism, a new fluorescence assay for the detection of alcohol content was established with a limit of detection as low as 0.12 %. This sensing platform possessed the advantages of low cost, portability, and simplicity of operation, and laid a further foundation for the on-site detection application of R-CDs in the field of sensing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent probe based on R-CDs was prepared by solvothermal method for the determination of alcohol content in alcoholic beverages.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Alcoholic beverages are a class of beverages that contain alcohol, including wine, beer, spirits, whiskey, vodka, and others [1]. The quality and taste of alcoholic beverages are directly influenced by different production processes and raw materials, and the alcohol content is one of the most important indicators of alcoholic beverage quality [2]. The European Union and the United States require that spirits (such as whisky) have an alcohol content of not less than 40 %, and China requires that the alcohol content of baijiu (a Chinese distilled spirit) must be not less than 35 % [3,4]. Additionally, these countries require that the alcohol content must be clearly indicated on the label of bottled spirits. Therefore, the rapid and accurate determination of alcohol content in alcoholic beverages is of great significance for quality control and supervision.

The determination of alcohol content has always been an important issue in the production of alcoholic beverages. Traditional detection methods, including the density bottle method and alcoholometer method, are usually laborious, time-consuming, and tedious, as the complex sample preparation process prior to sample analysis is required, which indirectly leads to the low accuracy of traditional analysis methods. In recent years, various instrumental detection methods for alcohol content have emerged, such as gas chromatography, spectrophotometry, microbial electrode, near-infrared spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy [5–9]. However, each analytical method has its own advantages and limitations. For example, gas chromatography has high separation efficiency, but it is expensive and complicated to operate. Although the microbial electrode method has good selectivity and reproducibility for tracking monitoring, its electrode life is usually short and the cost is high. Therefore, it is important to develop a rapid, simple, and accurate method for the detection of alcohol concentration. Fluorescence spectroscopy has attracted widespread attention due to its high sensitivity, wide linear range, and good selectivity [10]. It has broad application prospects in the fields of food, chemistry, and biology, and can be applied for rapid and accurate detection of additives, pollutants, nutrients, drugs, genes, and proteins [11–14]. In order to enhance the stability and sensitivity of fluorescence spectroscopy, numerous nanomaterials have been widely developed, such as carbon-based nanomaterials, metal nanomaterials, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [15–18]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) are novel carbon-based nanomaterials with strong fluorescence characteristics and unique physical and chemical properties [19–22]. Compared to traditional metal quantum dots, CDs have the advantages of simple preparation, low-cost raw materials, good biocompatibility, and flexible surface modification [23]. These perfect characteristics make CDs have broad application prospects in the fields of biomedicine, environmental monitoring, and chemical sensing [24–26]. Previous studies have reported the use of CDs as fluorescent probes to detect various substances, such as metal ions, small molecule compounds, and biomolecules. Unfortunately, most reported carbon dots emitted blue fluorescence, which often seriously interfered with the blue spontaneous fluorescence of complex matrices in biological and food systems [27,28]. Red emission CDs (R-CDs) have attracted increasing attention due to their advantages of long emission wavelengths, little matrix damage, and strong tissue penetration [29]. The development of an R-CDs-based fluorescent probe can provide better choices for the analysis and detection of various compounds in biological and food samples. However, to date, there have been no reports of using CDs as fluorescent probes to detect alcohol content in alcoholic beverages. Therefore, it has great application potential to develop a CD-based fluorescent sensor for rapid and accurate detection of alcohol content in alcoholic beverages.

In recent years, on-site detection and real-time analysis have played an increasingly important role in the field of analysis and detection [30]. Among them, colorimetry can achieve qualitative or quantitative analysis by observing color changes with the naked eye or photography and has become one of the most commonly used detection techniques. Due to the functionality of independent photography, image color analysis, and data processing in smartphones, smartphone photography colorimetric analysis can improve the sensitivity and accuracy of detection. It can be seen that the smartphone detection platform has great potential in the practical on-site detection of samples [31]. To achieve more accurate results, an innovative detection method was developed that employed three modes (fluorescence spectrophotometer, light-smartphone, and 365 nm UV lamp-smartphone) for the simultaneous detection of alcohol content.

Based on the above ideas, the novel reusable red-emission carbon dots (R-CDs) were prepared by a simple solvothermal method ( Scheme 1) using 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([OMIM]BF4), o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA), and 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (BTC) as precursors. The prepared CDs not only possessed the advantages of long-wavelength emission and superior fluorescence stability but also permitted multiple reuses. In addition, the R-CDs exhibited excellent dispersibility in ethanol and aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) in water. The alcohol content in four alcoholic beverages was rapidly and sensitively detected using a fluorescence spectrophotometer and a smartphone. Therefore, the established R-CDs-smartphone sensing platform not only met the demands of actual sample analysis but also provided support for further application in visual detection.

The 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([OMIM]BF4), ortho-phenylenediamine (o-PDA), and 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (BTC) were provided by Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose (Glu), maltose (Mal), fructose (Fru), quercetin (Quer), and tartaric acid (Tart) were purchased from Shanghai Yuan Ye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). KCl, NaCl, Al(OH)3·3H2O, CaCl2, MgCl2, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were provided by Beijing Chemical Plant (Beijing, China). Eight alcoholic beverage samples (whisky, vodka, tequila, baijiu, white wine, Rum, brandy and Sake) were purchased from a local supermarket (Changchun, China). Ultra-pure water was prepared using a Milli-Q water purification system.

The morphology study of R-CDs was conducted using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, JEM-2100F). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy was performed on an infrared spectrophotometer (Thermofisher, Nicolet IS50). Zeta potential was determined by a zeta potential analyzer (PSS, Nicomp 380). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded from 10° to 80° using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, SmartLab-9 kW). The elemental content and valence were obtained through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Malvern Panalytical, X‘Pert3 MRD). UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary50) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, LS-55), respectively. In addition, a visual detection platform was constructed using a UV analyzer (WD-9403F) and a smartphone (iPhone 13).

The CDs with red fluorescence emission were prepared by solvothermal method using [OMIM]BF4, o-PDA, and BTC as precursors. Specifically, 1.00 g of [OMIM]BF4, 0.25 g of o-PDA, 1.00 g of BTC, and 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol were added to a stainless steel reactor with a polytetrafluoroethylene lining, and the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 12 h under self-generated pressure. The mixture was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter, and the obtained supernatant was purified using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da for 12 h. Finally, the R-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying and stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

A series of ethanol solutions with concentrations of 0–100 % were prepared, into which 200 μL of R-CDs were added. After mixing, the emission spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 580 nm. Subsequently, the selectivity of R-CDs for different interferents was investigated, including K+, Na+, Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Glu, Mal, Fru, Quer, and Tart.

ce:section.ce:section-title: R-CDs- Smartphone detection platform for alcohol content
ce:section.ce:para: To start with, different concentrations of alcohol solutions (0–100 %) were mixed with R-CDs and the reaction solution was subsequently transferred to a cuvette. The cuvette and smartphone were positioned at a fixed location within the UV analyzer and incubated for 1 min. Under illumination and 360 nm UV radiation, fluorescence images were captured and processed using the smartphone app (Color Picker) and were analyzed by RGB values. The experiment was conducted repeatedly in triplicate for statistical analysis. The alcohol content of real samples was determined using the same procedure without sample treatment.

The morphology and dimensions of R-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1A and 1B, the R-CDs presented uniformly distributed spherical particles with an average diameter of 2.40 ± 0.58 nm calculated from 100 nanoparticles. The high-resolution TEM image displayed lattice fringes characterized by an average spacing of 0.20 nm. A broad diffraction peak was observed at 2θ = 20.8° in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1C), corresponding to the lattice structure of the (002) graphite plane [32]. The surface functional groups of R-CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 1D showed that stretching vibrations of C–H bonds were observed at 2930 cm−1 [33], an absorption peak corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO appeared at 1730 cm−1 [34], stretching vibrations of NO2 were detected at 1510 cm−1 [35], and stretching vibrations of C–F and C–O were observed at 1250 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1, respectively [36]. These findings indicated that the R-CDs possessed graphitic structure and exhibited hydrophobicity due to multiple hydrophobic functional groups on their surface, such as C–H, C–F, NO2, and C–O. These results were confirmed by the observed phenomena during subsequent practical experiments.

The electronic structure and surface elemental composition of R-CDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2A exhibited five peaks at 284.8 eV, 401.6 eV, 532.1 eV, 685.7 eV, and 193.9 eV, which corresponded to the C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, F 1 s, and B 1 s elements, respectively (Fig. 2A). The C 1 s spectrum could be resolved into three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.9 eV, indicating C–C, C–N, and C–O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2B) [37]. The N 1 s spectrum confirmed the existence of two nitrogen states, namely C–N (401.2 eV) and N–H (401.7 eV) (Fig. 2C) [38]. In the O 1 s spectrum, two different peaks corresponding to C–O and CO bonds appeared at 532.0 eV and 533.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 2D) [39]. The F 1 s spectral peaks at 685.5 eV and 686.1 eV were attributed to the characteristic peaks of semi-ionic and covalent C-F bonds (Fig. 2E). Additionally, peaks corresponding to B–C and B–O bonds appeared at 193.9 eV and 194.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 2F) [40]. XPS spectral analysis further demonstrated the results obtained from FT-IR spectroscopy, indicating the successful doping of N, F, and B atoms into R-CDs, which would help improve the photoluminescent properties of R-CDs.

The optical properties of R-CDs were investigated by fluorescence and UV–visible absorption spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3A, the R-CDs exhibited a prominent UV absorption peak at 245 nm, which was attributed to the π-π* electron transition of the CC bond. Additionally, the shoulder peak at 296 nm indicated the presence of n-π* electron transition of CO in the carbon dots [41]. The R-CDs presented red color under light and bright red fluorescence under a UV lamp (Inset in Fig. 3A). As shown in the fluorescence spectra, the R-CDs exhibited the optimal emission peak at 580 nm with excitation wavelength ranging from 530 nm to 590 nm and displayed excitation-independent characteristics (Fig. 3B). This fluorescence behavior suggested that the R-CDs had a nearly uniform surface and a similar bandgap. Moreover, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of R-CDs was measured to be 10.86 % and the average fluorescence lifetime was 1.32 ns determined from the time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. S1).

To ensure the accuracy and reliability of R-CDs in practical applications, the fluorescence stability of R-CDs was examined. The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was tested at different pH values. In strong acidic and basic solutions, the fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced due to the protonation and deprotonation of the organic functional groups on the surface [42]. However, within the pH range of 3.0–13.0, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs remained stable, indicating its ability to detect actual samples (Fig. S2). Further investigation on the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs and the concentration of NaCl in the solution and the UV irradiation time was conducted. The R-CDs could maintain high fluorescence intensity even when the NaCl concentration reached 0.1 mol/L (Fig. S3). After 10 min of UV lamp irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs remained unchanged, indicating its strong photobleaching resistance (Fig. S4). Additionally, the R-CDs solution was placed at room temperature (25 ℃) and low temperature (4 ℃) for 30 days, and no precipitation was observed. Finally, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs and temperature was studied. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the R-CDs still maintained strong fluorescence stability even in high temperature condition (80 °C), demonstrating their suitability for practical sample analysis environments. These results suggest that the R-CDs possessed good optical stability, especially with great potential in the field of analytical detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of alcohol content by R-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: To determine the alcohol content of alcoholic beverage samples, it is necessary to examine the status of R-CDs in ethanol and water. The R-CDs demonstrated remarkable dispersibility and emitted red fluorescence when dispersed in ethanol. However, when dispersed in water, they tended to aggregate, leading to the quenching of the red fluorescence ( Fig. 4A). Therefore, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching might be induced by aggregation [43]. For further verification, the UV absorption spectra of R-CDs before and after the addition of water were investigated. Compared with the UV spectra before the addition of water, no new absorption peaks were observed after the addition of water, indicating that no new chemical bonds formed between R-CDs (Fig. 4B). Therefore, the possibility of static quenching was excluded. Moreover, Zeta potential is a parameter that characterizes the surface charge state and charge distribution uniformity of particles. Generally, when the absolute value of the Zeta potential is greater than 30 mV, the particles are in a stable dispersed state, while when the absolute value is less than 10 mV, aggregation may occur. As shown in Fig. 4C, the zeta potentials of R-CDs in ethanol and water were −33.23 ± 1.56 mV and −8.22 ± 1.35 mV, respectively. In ethanol, the surface of R-CDs carried a strong negative charge. The significant electrostatic repulsion generated by this negative charge played a crucial role in upholding the dispersion stability of R-CDs in ethanol and inhibiting their aggregation. However, the surface negative charge of R-CDs diminished in water because water molecules adsorbed onto their surface and neutralized some of the negative charges. Consequently, electrostatic repulsion was weakened, making R-CDs more prone to mutual attraction and facilitating aggregation in water [44]. It could be observed from the TEM images that the size of R-CDs increased nearly 200-fold due to aggregation in water compared to in ethanol (Fig. 4D). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of R-CDs in water was caused by aggregation. Thus, the alcohol content in alcoholic beverages could be determined on basis of this mechanism.

To prevent interference from other substances in alcoholic beverage samples, the specificity of R-CDs towards water was investigated. Firstly, by adding a small amount of different solvents of the same volume to the CDs-ethanol solution, it was found that only water affected the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs (Fig. S6). Then, the potential effects of competing ions (K+, Na+, Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Mn2+) and the possible substances (Glu, Mal, Fru, Quer, and Tart) that might be present in alcoholic beverages on R-CDs were investigated in the CDs-ethanol-water system ( Fig. 5A). Meanwhile, the influence of interfering substances on the changes in fluorescence intensity was investigated at different concentrations, and it was found that all the interference signals were less than 3.0 % (Fig. S7). The above results indicated that the alcohol content could be accurately determined by the R-CDs in the presence of interfering substances.

The relationship between alcohol content (0–100 %) and the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was found to be positively correlated with alcohol content. Therefore, a regression equation of Y= 3.26 + 2.33exp(0.05X) was derived, demonstrating remarkable goodness of fit to the experimental data (R2 = 0.9998, P > F = 6.11091E-17). Furthermore, based on the average value of 20 blank samples plus three times the average standard deviation [45], the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.12 %, which was lower than previous reports (Table S1). Compared to other studies, this method had a broader linear range and lower detection limits. More importantly, the R-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity, enabling the accurate determination of alcohol content not only in organic solvents but also in alcoholic beverage samples. These advantages provided the possibility for the economic and efficient detection of alcohol content in alcoholic beverage samples.

The reaction time and reusability of R-CDs were investigated before the actual sample analysis. The aggregation of R-CDs in water could rapidly complete within 1 min, providing a possibility for on-site and real-time detection of alcohol content (Fig. S8). In addition, the R-CDs were well dispersed in ethanol, but aggregated at the bottom of water, leading to fluorescence quenching. However, after replacing the solvent with ethanol, the ACQ mechanism disappeared, and the R-CDs could be re-dispersed and emit fluorescence again (Fig. S9). After 6 cycles, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs remained above 90 %, indicating their good reusability (Fig. S10). This laid the foundation for the economic practicality of R-CDs and was of great significance for actual sample analysis.

To validate the feasibility of the method, the matrix effect was first examined. Statistical analysis using a t-test revealed no significant difference between the standard curve method and the working curve method at a 95 % confidence level. Therefore, in this study, the standard curve method was adopted for the analysis of actual samples. The results demonstrated that the errors in determining the alcohol content in actual alcohol samples by R-CDs were between 0.1 % and 0.4 %, indicating the high accuracy and applicability of the present method ( Table 1). Each sample was measured in triplicate, and the calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3 %, demonstrating the good precision of this method. Overall, the analysis results suggested that R-CDs had excellent analytical performance and could effectively measure the alcohol content in actual samples.

Smartphone imaging was gradually becoming an important tool in the field of analytical detection, as it could achieve functions such as image acquisition, signal detection, and quantitative analysis. When quantitatively detecting the alcohol content in alcoholic beverage samples, the reaction solution was placed in a cuvette, and photos were taken using a smartphone camera under light and a 365 nm UV lamp ( Fig. 6). Subsequently, the color of the photo was converted to RGB values through smartphone APP (Color picker). As shown in Fig. 6C and D, by analyzing the relationship between the RGB values and the alcohol content in the reaction solution, the following linear relationships could be obtained: under light, R/B = 0.2076 + 0.0277X (R² = 0.9946, P > F = 9.74466E-12); under UV lamp, R/B = 0.2586 + 0.0068X (R² = 0.9952, P > F = 5.8525E-12). The LODs for the two conditions were calculated using 3σ/K, resulting in values of 0.19 % and 0.16 %, respectively. The results of the actual sample analysis were also satisfactorily verified (Table S2). These results indicated that the use of an R-CDs-smartphone detection platform for real-time and on-site quantitative detection of alcohol content was feasible. In addition, the color change could be made into a color chart for a rough estimation of the alcohol content through visual comparison. Highly sensitive detection of alcohol content could be achieved by all three modes (fluorescence spectrophotometer, light-smartphone, 365 nm UV lamp-smartphone). The simultaneous utilization of these three modes could further ensure the accuracy of the detection.

A novel sensing platform was developed for the rapid and highly sensitive determination of alcohol content in alcoholic beverage samples. The synthesized R-CDs exhibited outstanding optical properties and were found to be reusable, highlighting their potential for use in various applications. The determination of alcohol content was achieved by utilizing the aggregation-caused quenching phenomenon of R-CDs induced by the presence of water. The present method demonstrated satisfactory linearity, low limit of detection, and high selectivity. The results of the actual sample analysis were accurate with good precision. In addition, the on-site detection of alcohol content could be achieved by constructing an R-CDs-smartphone detection platform, which provided a reliable platform for alcohol content sensing. In summary, the present sensing platform not only enabled real-time and reliable determination of alcohol content but also provided a new pathway for on-site analysis.

Chenzhao Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Writing-original draft. Junxian Li: Methodology, Validation. Xiujuan Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Zhenjie Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Runxiang Yao: Methodology, Validation. Yuhao Jiang: Methodology, Validation. Shanshan Wei: Methodology, Validation. Zhibing Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Supervision, Validation, Writing-original draft. Guoying Sun: Conceptualization, Data curation, Supervision, Validation, Writing-original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation (20220101350JC) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2241287).

IEEE Trans. Instrum. Meas.

J. Chromatogr. A

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chenzhao Wang is a Ph.D. candidate in Analytical Chemistry at Changchun University of Technology. His research focuses on the preparation and application of optical sensors. Under the supervision of Dr. Zhibing Wang, he is actively involved in advancing the field of analytical chemistry and optical sensor technology. Chenzhao Wang has a strong academic background and is dedicated to conducting innovative research in the field of analytical chemistry. His current research work involves the development of novel materials for optical sensors and their practical applications. Through his studies, he aims to contribute to the advancement of optical sensing technologies and their potential applications in various fields.

Dr. Zhibing Wang serves as the corresponding author for this publication. He is an experienced researcher in the field of analytical chemistry and specializes in the fabrication and application of optical sensors. He can be contacted at wangzhibing@ccut.edu.cn.
